[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: cancelRoot] Can an unprivileged attacker who is Midnight-authorized by the maker (via IMidnight.setIsAuthorized) call cancelRoot(maker, root) to permanently invalidate every offer in the maker's signed Merkle tree, under the precondition that the maker has granted coarse-grained authorization to any address (e.g., a callback contract, a periphery router, or a test operator), triggering the call sequence: attacker calls midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, maker) [if maker authorized attacker] then attacker calls ecrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root), causing all subsequent midnight.take(offer, ratifierData, ...) calls for any offer in that tree to revert with RootCanceled(), violating the invariant that a maker's valid signed offer tree must remain fillable until expiry or explicit maker cancellation, causing scoped impact: permanent low-cost DoS on take for all offers in the canceled tree? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: isRatified + HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash] Can a malicious taker supply ratifierData with proof.length == 21 (or any value > 20) to cause HashLib.offerTreeTypeHash to revert with TreeTooHigh inside isRatified, under the precondition that the taker constructs ratifierData with a proof array of length 21 that passes the HashLib.isLeaf check (using a valid 21-level Merkle path to a root that matches a canceled or arbitrary root), triggering the call sequence: taker calls midnight.take(offer, ratifierData_with_21_proof_elements, ...) → isRatified decodes proof.length=21 → isLeaf passes → offerTreeTypeHash(21) reverts with TreeTooHigh → take reverts, violating the invariant that a taker cannot cause take to revert with an unexpected error by manipulating ratifierData, causing scoped impact: self-DoS or griefing if taker is a contract that expects specific revert reasons? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: isRatified + abi.decode] Can a malicious taker supply malformed ratifierData that causes abi.decode to revert inside isRatified, under the precondition that the taker passes ratifierData that is not a valid ABI encoding of (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[]), triggering the call sequence: taker calls midnight.take(offer, malformed_ratifierData, ...) → isRatified calls abi.decode(ratifierData, (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[])) → revert → take reverts, violating the invariant that a taker cannot cause take to revert with an ABI decode panic by supplying arbitrary ratifierData, causing scoped impact: self-DoS for taker, but also potential griefing if taker is an authorized operator acting on behalf of a borrower? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Denial of service against core market actions with low attacker cost, especially take, repay, withdraw, liquidate, or claim fee'] [Function: cancelRoot + multi-offer tree] Can an attacker cancel a single Merkle root to simultaneously DoS all N offers in a tree of up to 2^20 offers, under the precondition that a maker has published a large Merkle tree of offers (e.g., offers across multiple markets, ticks, and maturities) all sharing one signed root, triggering the call sequence: attacker (authorized by maker) calls ecrecoverRatifier.cancelRoot(maker, root) once → all 2^20 offers in the tree become permanently unfillable, violating the invariant that cancellation of one offer must not affect other independent offers, causing scoped impact: single-transaction DoS of an entire offer book at O(1) cost?
